beads: file ft-btg0h (frankenterm/term Terminal::advance_bytes fuzz target)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

@@ -116,6 +116,7 @@
 {"id":"ft-b5rbd","title":"[HIGH] GUI resize increment saturation can overflow backend min-size arithmetic","description":"Commit 193a9ebb changes `ResizeIncrementCalculator` to saturate `base_width`/`base_height` at the u16 ceiling (`crates/frankenterm-gui/src/resize_increment_calculator.rs:15-35`). Multiple window backends then immediately compute `base + increment` in u16 without guarding overflow (`frankenterm/window/src/os/macos/window.rs:1410-1412`, `frankenterm/window/src/os/x11/window.rs:1892-1893`, `frankenterm/window/src/os/wayland/window.rs:915-916`). With large padding/border/tab-bar geometry, the new saturation can produce `base_width == u16::MAX`, so `base_width + x` overflows before min-size clamping. This turns an intended overflow hardening change into a runtime panic/wraparound path on oversized layouts. Expected fix: clamp stored base dimensions to `u16::MAX - increment` (or widen backend arithmetic) and add a regression test that asserts `base + increment` stays representable.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T05:58:04.554054Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T06:42:08.945504Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T06:42:08.945232Z","close_reason":"dup of ft-htf0i","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-b677n","title":"[MEDIUM] wgpu readback poll loop has no test coverage for timeout, disconnect, or error-propagation paths","description":"Commit 6f41130c (br-43bbl fix wgpu readback polling) rewrites the
readback wait in crates/frankenterm-gui/src/termwindow/webgpu.rs:168-193
from:

\`\`\`rust
self.device.poll(PollType::Wait { submission_index: None, timeout: Some(WEBGPU_READBACK_TIMEOUT) })?;
receiver.recv_timeout(WEBGPU_READBACK_TIMEOUT)?...
\`\`\`

to a bounded poll loop:

\`\`\`rust
let deadline = Instant::now() + WEBGPU_READBACK_TIMEOUT;
loop {
    self.device.poll(wgpu::PollType::Poll)?;
    match receiver.recv_timeout(Duration::from_millis(10)) {
        Ok(result) => { result?; break; }
        Err(RecvTimeoutError::Timeout) if Instant::now() < deadline => continue,
        Err(RecvTimeoutError::Timeout) => return Err(...),
        Err(RecvTimeoutError::Disconnected) => return Err(...),
    }
}
\`\`\`

Concerns with the new implementation:

1. \`PollType::Poll\` is non-blocking by contract in wgpu 25. The loop
   relies on \`Poll\` actually driving the \`map_async\` callback forward.
   If a future wgpu point release changes \`Poll\` to be strictly
   \"dispatch-only\" (no callback invocation), the loop would always
   hit the 5s timeout. There is no test pinning this behavior.

2. The \`Disconnected\` branch is reachable only if the \`map_async\`
   closure drops without sending — that happens e.g., if wgpu drops
   the submission without invoking the callback (device lost, internal
   error). No test exercises this path.

3. \`Instant::now() + WEBGPU_READBACK_TIMEOUT\` panics on overflow
   (impossible in practice for 5s, but worth noting as a latent
   concern). Prefer \`checked_add\`.

4. Busy-wait cost: on the timeout path, the loop spins ~500 iterations
   (5000ms / 10ms) calling \`device.poll\` each time. The prior
   \`PollType::Wait\` blocked in the driver. This is a CPU-efficiency
   regression on cancellation/device-loss paths, not a correctness
   regression.

Impact: readback behavior on wgpu failure paths (device lost, long
delay, map_async disconnect) is silently unverified. A correctness
regression in \`PollType::Poll\` semantics between wgpu versions would
silently break screenshots and readback-based features.

Fix direction:
- Add a unit-level test with a mock/stub Device that controls
  \`poll()\` return timing and can force a Disconnected on the mpsc
  channel. Assert each of the four loop outcomes.
- Consider moving the readback loop into a small helper function
  that takes a \`Fn()\` poll closure + channel receiver, to make it
  unit-testable without a real GPU.
- If unit isolation is impractical, at minimum add an integration
  test that triggers a readback and asserts completion within a
  tight CPU budget (regression test for busy-wait blow-up).

Severity: medium — latent risk in a GPU-facing path that typically
works and has no automated coverage for failure modes.

Evidence: commit 6f41130c diff;
crates/frankenterm-gui/src/termwindow/webgpu.rs:168-193 at HEAD.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T07:25:33.419495Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T08:48:13.062749Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T08:48:13.062383Z","close_reason":"Added timeout/disconnect/cancel-style readback coverage and hardened the helper deadline with checked_add","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["gpu","review","test-gap","testing","webgpu"]}
 {"id":"ft-bs0ec","title":"robot_types agent inventory golden contract","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","assignee":"pane8","created_at":"2026-04-22T04:22:08.228201Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T09:56:43.693909Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T09:56:43.693614Z","close_reason":"Landed in 97ec6350 (test(frankenterm-core): freeze agent_inventory_contract.json golden for robot payload)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"ft-btg0h","title":"[fuzz] add frankenterm/term Terminal::advance_bytes target for full escape-to-state pipeline","description":"Surface: frankenterm/term/src/terminal.rs:164 — pub fn advance_bytes<B: AsRef<[u8]>>(&mut self, bytes: B). Master terminal state-machine entry point: raw output bytes → frankenterm_escape_parser → Performer::perform (terminalstate/performer.rs:252) → every CSI/SGR/OSC/DCS dispatch path (perform_csi_mode/window/edit/cursor/sgr in terminalstate/mod.rs:1425-2648, plus image pipelines in terminalstate/sixel.rs, terminalstate/kitty.rs (37KB), terminalstate/iterm.rs). Existing escape_parser_raw fuzz target covers only the lexer stage (bytes → Action); termwiz_csi_parser covers the escape-parser CSI roundtrip property. Nothing exercises the state-machine mutations, cursor/edit bounds, or the Sixel/Kitty/iTerm2 image decoders under arbitrary input.

Proposed harness: fuzz/fuzz_targets/term_advance_bytes.rs — construct a Terminal with a minimal TerminalConfiguration impl, 24x80 size, cap input at 256 KB, feed the whole slice through advance_bytes, drop the terminal. No assertions — libFuzzer asserts no-panic / no-OOM / no-unbounded-alloc.

Corpus seeds: plain text, ESC[H clear, SGR sequences, DECSET/DECRST modes, DECRQM, OSC 52 clipboard, OSC 8 hyperlinks, bracketed paste, CSI u keyboard, DCS sixel fragment (\\x1bPq#0;2;100;0;0#0~~\\x1b\\\\), kitty APC image fragment, iTerm2 OSC 1337 file upload preamble, overlong / truncated / interleaved-with-text variants.

Target must survive 256 KB input without: panic, unbounded allocation (scrollback bounded at 64), unterminated DCS hang, arithmetic overflow in cursor arithmetic, OOB cell index. This discovers sixel/kitty image-decoder arithmetic bugs and any CSI dispatch path that does not clamp its operand to grid bounds.

Add to fuzz/Cargo.toml: frankenterm-term = { path = "../frankenterm/term" }. No new features required — Terminal::advance_bytes is in the crate public API for terminal hosts.

Do NOT duplicate: ft-t4n0z (termwiz InputParser, closed), ft-he0w7 (native_events, closed), ft-p2bjm (replay, in flight), escape_parser_raw (lexer stage only), termwiz_csi_parser (CSI roundtrip property on escape-parser crate, not term::Terminal).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-23T07:08:20.022220Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T07:08:20.022220Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fuzz","security"]}
 {"id":"ft-btls2","title":"recorder config validation must reject unimplemented frankensqlite backend","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane8","created_at":"2026-04-22T04:20:11.800729Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T04:37:48.708070Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T04:37:48.707749Z","close_reason":"fixed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-bvrjc","title":"tmux split command failure leaves pending split future unresolved","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane8","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:17:18.852994Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:33:39.856264Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:33:39.855284Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 65094825. Verified via FT_LOCAL_TARGET=/tmp/ft-rv-p8-bvrjc-target ./scripts/cargo-local.sh test -p mux --lib split_command_error_resolves_pending_split_with_error -- --nocapture. Full mux --lib remains red on ft-3ek7f (domain::tests::local_domain_spawn_pane_first_poll_stays_non_blocking).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-bx4le","title":"[bug] SignalDeduplicator silently behaves like capacity=1 when dedup_capacity=0 (connector_inbound_bridge)","description":"# Bug

connector_inbound_bridge.rs:338 SignalDeduplicator::check_and_record:

    if self.entries.len() >= self.capacity {
        self.entries.pop_front();
    }
    self.entries.push_back(...);

With `dedup_capacity = 0`:
  1. Entry 1: len=0 >= 0 true → pop_front (no-op on empty) → push_back → entries=[a]
  2. Entry 2: len=1 >= 0 true → pop_front removes [a] → push_back → entries=[b]
  3. ...each call mutates entries but len stays at 1.

Net: dedup silently operates as if capacity=1 (one-slot cache that
replaces on every call), not as "dedup disabled."

# Semantic gap

The `dedup_capacity: usize` config field advertises no special
value. Operator setting `dedup_capacity = 0` in their ft.toml
reasonably expects EITHER:
  - dedup disabled (every signal treated as new)
  - config rejected at load

Instead: dedup cache degrades to a 1-entry replacer. Every signal
is compared against only the immediately-preceding signal. Duplicate
detection is 99.99% broken, but no warning fires.

# Parallel precedent

1ad5c8cc fixed the EXACT same class in ConnectorOutboundBridge:
  `dispatch_queue_capacity == 0` now fails-fast with a distinct
  telemetry label and blocked-action record.

The inbound dedup analog needs the same treatment — short-circuit
on `capacity == 0` and bypass the dedup check, returning `true`
unconditionally (every signal is "new") so the bridge doesn't
silently corrupt its dedup semantics.

# Fix

    pub fn check_and_record(&mut self, correlation_id: &str, now_ms: u64) -> bool {
        // [ft-*] When capacity=0, dedup is effectively disabled —
        // skip all tracking and treat every signal as new. Without
        // this, the `len >= 0` check below degrades the cache to
        // a 1-slot replacer rather than honoring the "no dedup"
        // intent.
        if self.capacity == 0 {
            return true;
        }
        self.evict_expired(now_ms);
        // ... existing logic ...
    }

One test: `signal_deduplicator_zero_capacity_bypasses_dedup` —
push N entries with same correlation_id, assert all return `true`
and `len() == 0`.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:33:16.059276Z","created_by":"pane3-inbound-zero","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:42:16.478614Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:42:16.478198Z","close_reason":"Landed in 8fdfd114 (fix: six independent hardening fixes … connector_inbound_bridge zero-capacity dedup bypass). SignalDeduplicator::check_and_record now short-circuits with true when capacity=0, preserving the 'dedup disabled' semantic operators expect. Companion regression test dedup_zero_capacity_bypasses_dedup pins 5-call same-id + multi-id paths. Verified: 1 passed / 0 failed / 22001 filtered (cargo test --lib --features asupersync-runtime -- dedup_zero_capacity), EXIT=0. Parallel to 1ad5c8cc outbound zero-capacity fix.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["connector","inbound-bridge","zero-capacity"]}
@@ -240,7 +241,7 @@
 {"id":"ft-qgk35","title":"[mor:security-engineering] wa.send trust boundary is 'which agent', not 'what bytes'","description":"# Observation

`WaSendTool` (mcp_tools.rs:1819) caps payload at 4 MiB (ft-05hfm) and
gates via the policy engine. Everything the authenticated agent
submits as \`text\` is then forwarded through
\`wezterm cli send-text -- <text>\` to the target pane.

The Command-args layering is safe from shell injection: args are
passed directly, not through \`sh -c\`. But send-text is typing
bytes into a terminal, and those bytes include the full control-char
space:

- \\x03 (Ctrl+C) kills the foreground process
- \\x04 (Ctrl+D) sends EOF, often exiting shells/REPLs
- \\x1a (Ctrl+Z) suspends
- Escape sequences can fake prompt text (`\\x1b[H\\x1b[2J$ `)
- Bracketed-paste wrappers can be forged

Trust model today: "if the agent is authenticated and policy allows
scope, arbitrary bytes are fine." This is appropriate for the
normal case (an agent typing \`npm test\` into its own pane). It is
less obviously appropriate for:

- agent A sending into agent B's pane (policy gates scope but not
  content)
- a compromised/misconfigured agent signing off its scope with a
  ghost Ctrl+C
- typing \`\\x1bc\` (reset terminal) into a neighbor pane to mask
  the audit trail of whatever just scrolled off

# Question

Would a lightweight content class — "does this payload contain
control characters outside the normal typing range?" — as a
separate policy capability be worth the complexity? The default
policy could allow control chars (current behavior); a hardening
toggle could require an explicit \`send-control-chars\` capability.

Agents that need to Ctrl+C their own long-running commands would
declare the capability up front; the 99% of typing-plain-text cases
would run without it and be structurally incapable of sending
Ctrl+C into another pane.

# Trust boundary

Current boundary: authenticated identity + policy scope + size cap.
Open question: should the boundary also include "which byte classes
can flow"? For security platforms, the answer is usually yes; the
tradeoff is ergonomics cost during normal agent operation.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:35:28.713563Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-23T00:18:49.541886Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T18:02:30.134277Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mcp","mor","policy","security"],"comments":[{"id":3601,"issue_id":"ft-qgk35","author":"content-trust-audit","text":"answered — investigation found content-aware gating already exists via PolicyEngine::analyze_content_risk (policy.rs:4792) + PredicateKind::CommandPattern (policy.rs:3296). Trust boundary IS identity + bytes, not identity alone. Specific extension to cover terminal-control bytes (Ctrl+C/D/Z, ESC, bracketed-paste wraps) filed as child ft-qgk35.1 (P2) with ~10-line patch + 5 tests.","created_at":"2026-04-21T18:02:57Z"}]}
 {"id":"ft-qgk35.1","title":"[security] wa.send: extend analyze_content_risk to flag terminal-control bytes","description":"# Parent

ft-qgk35 asked whether wa.send's trust boundary should include "what
bytes flow," not just "which agent is authenticated." Investigation
found the policy engine **already has content-aware gating** — the
question collapses from "should we add a mechanism?" to "should we
extend the existing mechanism to cover a specific blind spot?"

# Mechanism already in place

`PolicyEngine::analyze_content_risk` (crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs:4792)
runs on every `ActionKind::SendText` with non-empty `command_text`.
It flags:

- **content.destructive_tokens** (40 pts): rm -rf, DROP TABLE, dd if=, …
- **content.sudo_elevation** (30 pts): sudo / doas / run0
- **content.multiline_complex** (15 pts): multi-line heredoc
- **content.pipe_chain** (10 pts): ≥ 2 pipes

And `PredicateKind::CommandPattern { patterns }`
(crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs:3296) lets operators write
regex-match rules on `command_text`.

So content-aware gating **exists**. The parent bead (ft-qgk35)'s
framing of "trust boundary is identity, not bytes" is incorrect —
the boundary already inspects bytes for a specific class of risks.

# The actual gap

`analyze_content_risk` is **shell-command aware** (rm -rf, sudo,
pipes) but not **terminal-control aware**. It does not flag:

- \x03 (Ctrl+C) — can kill another agent's foreground process
- \x04 (Ctrl+D) — can exit another agent's REPL/shell
- \x1a (Ctrl+Z) — can background another agent's work
- \x1b prefix (escape sequences) — can fake prompts, scramble
  the scrollback, clear the screen to mask evidence
- Bracketed-paste wrappers (\x1b[200~ ... \x1b[201~) — can be
  forged to make injected text read as human-pasted by agent
  detection heuristics

On the fleet of 200+ panes described in README.md, a compromised or
misconfigured agent that has scope to wa.send into a neighbor pane
today has unrestricted ability to type control bytes. The risk
surface is not hypothetical — agent A releasing its reservation on
pane B via a ghost Ctrl+C is a known class of coordination failure
in multi-agent terminal systems.

# Proposed tightening

Add a new risk factor to `analyze_content_risk`:

```rust
// Terminal-control bytes
let has_control_byte = text.bytes().any(|b|
    b == 0x03 || b == 0x04 || b == 0x1a  // Ctrl+C, Ctrl+D, Ctrl+Z
    || b == 0x1b                           // ESC (escape sequences)
);
if has_control_byte {
    self.add_factor(
        factors,
        "content.terminal_control_bytes",
        RiskCategory::Content,
        25,  // between multiline (15) and sudo (30)
        "Payload contains terminal-control bytes (Ctrl+C/D/Z or ESC)",
    );
}
```

Point value 25 chosen so default `risk_config.allow_max` still
permits typical interactive use (which should not contain control
bytes; agents that type `npm test` don't include \x03). Agents that
DO need Ctrl+C for long-running workflows trip the factor and
either:
  - pass through (if policy's allow_max accommodates 25), or
  - land in require-approval (if allow_max is below 25).

Either way the event is **visible in the audit trail** — which is
the actual ask from the parent bead.

# Alternative (higher-cost)

Introduce `PaneCapability::AcceptControlBytes` as a separate gate.
Richer but requires:
  - schema change to PaneCapabilities
  - migration for existing PaneState rows
  - config surface for operators to declare which panes accept
    control bytes

The risk-factor approach above is a ~10-line change with no schema
impact; the capability approach is ~150 lines and a migration. Pick
risk-factor unless a future bead explicitly wants per-pane
structural gating.

# Tests that should land with the fix

1. `analyze_content_risk_flags_ctrl_c`: "npm test\x03" triggers
   terminal_control_bytes factor.
2. `analyze_content_risk_plain_text_unchanged`: "npm test" does NOT
   trigger it (false-positive guard on printable ASCII).
3. `analyze_content_risk_flags_esc_prefix`: "\x1bc" (reset
   terminal) triggers.
4. `analyze_content_risk_flags_bracketed_paste_wrap`: the literal
   bytes `\x1b[200~...\x1b[201~` trigger (agent forging a paste).
5. `wa_send_dry_run_reports_control_byte_risk`: end-to-end from
   the MCP tool layer; dry-run with a Ctrl+C payload surfaces the
   factor in the injection preview.

# Disposition on parent

ft-qgk35 can be closed as `answered`: the mechanism exists; this
sub-bead tracks the specific extension. Keeping ft-qgk35 open risks
re-discovery of the same insight.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T18:02:15.998497Z","created_by":"content-trust-audit","updated_at":"2026-04-21T20:36:44.699744Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T20:36:44.698647Z","close_reason":"Verified in 010bac78","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mcp","policy","security","wa-send"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-qgk35.1","depends_on_id":"ft-qgk35","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-21T18:02:15.998497Z","created_by":"content-trust-audit","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"ft-qhyfh","title":"[LOW][review] user_packs discovery skips sandbox_resolve_dir (ft-6mxfs follow-up)","description":"# Observation

From a review pass over 10 swarm commits in the last 2 hours (git log --since='2 hours ago'), specifically the ft-6mxfs canonicalization fix (0239fbd6).

## Context

ft-6mxfs closed a directory-boundary symlink escape in `.ft/patterns` discovery by routing through `sandbox_resolve_dir(&ws_dir, root_path)` before walking. The fix is correct and well-tested.

## The gap

patterns.rs:938 — when `config.user_packs_enabled = true`, the code takes a different branch:

```rust
if config.user_packs_enabled {
    if let Some(user_dir) = config.resolved_user_packs_dir() {
        let discovered = discover_packs_from_dir(&user_dir)?;
        ...
    }
}
```

This call does NOT route through `sandbox_resolve_dir`. The user-packs dir is either the user-configured `user_packs_dir` (tilde-expanded) or `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/patterns`.

## Threat-model delta

Unlike `.ft/patterns` (inside a cloned repo — attacker-controlled), `~/.config/wa/patterns/` is under the user's own control. So the escape window is narrower — the user would have to set up the symlink themselves, at which point they already have write access.

However:
- If the user shares dotfiles across machines and a symlink in their config dir points at something sensitive on one machine but not another, a stray `wa` invocation could read files outside the intended scope.
- The inner per-file `sandbox_resolve` (ft-fbee5) still provides a per-YAML defense, so this is defense-in-depth, not a primary vulnerability.

## Proposed fix

Call `sandbox_resolve_dir(&user_dir, &user_dir.parent().unwrap_or(&user_dir))` or pass an explicit `user_sandbox_root` so the canonicalized-inside-root invariant extends to the user-packs path. Keep the behavior that a missing directory is Ok(None) (already the common case).

## Review context

Found via multi-pass review after shipping ft-h9g0q follow-up (retry.rs PaneNotFound classification). Rating LOW because it requires the user to be the attacker-of-themselves.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T20:39:18.533748Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T20:48:37.894024Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T20:48:37.893825Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
-{"id":"ft-qiba0","title":"[conformance] wa.mission_* / wa.tx_* (9 tools) lack schema+envelope golden roundtrip","description":"# Gap

Nine transactional tools in `tests/fixtures/mcp_manifest.json` have manifest-freeze coverage (schema gate, additionalProperties=false) but no envelope roundtrip conformance test:

* `wa.mission_state` — MissionRunState enum serialization, agent/action assignment maps
* `wa.mission_abort` — abort outcome envelope, idempotency on already-aborted missions
* `wa.mission_explain` — explain payload shape, trace graph
* `wa.mission_pause` / `wa.mission_resume` — lifecycle state-machine transitions
* `wa.tx_plan` — MissionTxContract shape, precondition list, classification field
* `wa.tx_run` — phase state transitions, compensating action record
* `wa.tx_show` — include_contract flag behaviour, chain verification data
* `wa.tx_rollback` — rollback outcome record

## Fix shape

Follow the same pattern as `mcp_conformance_rules_list_*` tests at mcp_conformance.rs:237-294 + the `agent_inventory_contract.json` golden shipped under ft-bs0ec (97ec6350). Recommended layout:

1. Fixture JSON per tool under `tests/fixtures/golden_robot_envelope/wa_{tool}.json` — canonical success envelope plus one denial/invalid-args envelope.
2. Test module `mcp_conformance_mission_tx.rs` that calls each tool with deterministic seeded inputs, canonicalises the response (BTreeMap ordering, strip volatile timestamps), and golden-compares.
3. Reuse helpers: `assert_common_envelope_fields`, `assert_success_envelope_shape`, `assert_object_matches_documented_schema`, plus `UPDATE_GOLDEN=1` regen wiring from `agent_inventory_golden.rs`.

## Why this matters

Mission + Tx are the load-bearing transactional surface (precondition check, rollback, chain verification). A silent envelope drift here corrupts downstream client decision paths worse than a UI-level surface drift would — the transactional semantics are what callers depend on to unwind on failure. The mission_contract_golden.rs at line 80589845 pins MissionTxContract serde roundtrip but NOT the MCP envelope that wraps it.

## Scope guard

9 goldens + 9 tests + regen harness. ~500 LOC. No production changes unless drift is found during fixture capture — in which case a separate bug bead would be filed per drift.

## Explicitly out of scope

Core operator tools (wa.search/get_text/send/wait_for) — tracked under the separate core-tools conformance bead. Events/cass families (wa.events_*, wa.cass_*) — lower operator stakes; can wait for a later pass.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-23T05:50:18.619029Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T05:54:45.510011Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"ft-qiba0","title":"[conformance] wa.mission_* / wa.tx_* (9 tools) lack schema+envelope golden roundtrip","description":"# Gap

Nine transactional tools in `tests/fixtures/mcp_manifest.json` have manifest-freeze coverage (schema gate, additionalProperties=false) but no envelope roundtrip conformance test:

* `wa.mission_state` — MissionRunState enum serialization, agent/action assignment maps
* `wa.mission_abort` — abort outcome envelope, idempotency on already-aborted missions
* `wa.mission_explain` — explain payload shape, trace graph
* `wa.mission_pause` / `wa.mission_resume` — lifecycle state-machine transitions
* `wa.tx_plan` — MissionTxContract shape, precondition list, classification field
* `wa.tx_run` — phase state transitions, compensating action record
* `wa.tx_show` — include_contract flag behaviour, chain verification data
* `wa.tx_rollback` — rollback outcome record

## Fix shape

Follow the same pattern as `mcp_conformance_rules_list_*` tests at mcp_conformance.rs:237-294 + the `agent_inventory_contract.json` golden shipped under ft-bs0ec (97ec6350). Recommended layout:

1. Fixture JSON per tool under `tests/fixtures/golden_robot_envelope/wa_{tool}.json` — canonical success envelope plus one denial/invalid-args envelope.
2. Test module `mcp_conformance_mission_tx.rs` that calls each tool with deterministic seeded inputs, canonicalises the response (BTreeMap ordering, strip volatile timestamps), and golden-compares.
3. Reuse helpers: `assert_common_envelope_fields`, `assert_success_envelope_shape`, `assert_object_matches_documented_schema`, plus `UPDATE_GOLDEN=1` regen wiring from `agent_inventory_golden.rs`.

## Why this matters

Mission + Tx are the load-bearing transactional surface (precondition check, rollback, chain verification). A silent envelope drift here corrupts downstream client decision paths worse than a UI-level surface drift would — the transactional semantics are what callers depend on to unwind on failure. The mission_contract_golden.rs at line 80589845 pins MissionTxContract serde roundtrip but NOT the MCP envelope that wraps it.

## Scope guard

9 goldens + 9 tests + regen harness. ~500 LOC. No production changes unless drift is found during fixture capture — in which case a separate bug bead would be filed per drift.

## Explicitly out of scope

Core operator tools (wa.search/get_text/send/wait_for) — tracked under the separate core-tools conformance bead. Events/cass families (wa.events_*, wa.cass_*) — lower operator stakes; can wait for a later pass.","status":"blocked","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-23T05:50:18.619029Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T07:04:19.440844Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-qzunb","title":"[HIGH] mux layout_arrangement + swap_layout JSON proptests fail on f64 ratio precision drift","description":"layout_arrangement_json_roundtrip and swap_layout_json_roundtrip in frankenterm/mux/tests/proptest_tab_wire_serde.rs currently fail at HEAD. arb_layout_arrangement generates LayoutArrangement::Split { ratio: 0.0f64..=1.0f64, ... } with recursive depth. When that ratio is serialized via serde_json and parsed back, some unrounded f64 values lose precision in the last decimal place (e.g. 0.10814827875804041 → 0.1081482787580404 and 0.9177432348398312 → 0.9177432348398313), so the roundtrip prop_assert_eq!(back, value) fails.

This is a blocker: the mux crate's cargo test currently shows 2/9 failing.

Fix direction: restrict the ratio generator to values that are exactly representable in IEEE 754 f64 with decimal notation (e.g. rounded to 1/2^16 fractions, or integer tenths/hundredths). Preferred: generate as integer 0..=10_000 and divide by 10_000.0 so every ratio is of the form n/10_000 and survives JSON's decimal representation. Keep the recursive depth/size parameters as-is.

Repro:
  CC=/opt/homebrew/opt/llvm/bin/clang CXX=/opt/homebrew/opt/llvm/bin/clang++ \
    CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/ft-pane6-target \
    cargo test --manifest-path frankenterm/mux/Cargo.toml --test proptest_tab_wire_serde

Observed at HEAD (04dc8d76).","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T17:59:44.814922Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T18:01:15.542629Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T18:01:15.542244Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 10309c8e: constrain arb_layout_arrangement's ratio to (0..=10_000).map(|n| n as f64 / 10_000) so every generated ratio is of the form n/10_000 and survives a serde_json decimal roundtrip bit-for-bit. All 10 tests in frankenterm/mux/tests/proptest_tab_wire_serde.rs now pass (was 7 pass / 2 fail). Granularity 0.0001 is more than adequate for split layout wire data.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["codec","mux","proptest","regression","serde"]}

 {"id":"ft-tzwuw","title":"mcp_tools ca.search/ca.view/ca.status accept timeout_secs=0, fire immediate-timeout","description":"## Defect

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs\` cass tools (ca.search line
590, ca.view line 683, ca.status line 763) all pass
\`params.timeout_secs\` directly to \`cass_client_with_timeout()\` without
validating that it's >= 1.

The JSON-Schema declarations for these tools advertise
\`"timeout_secs": { "type": "integer", "minimum": 1, "maximum": 600,
"default": 15 }\` (lines 526, 636, 726), but \`serde_json::from_value\`
does not enforce JSON-Schema bounds — it happily deserializes \`0\` as
a valid u64.

## Concrete failure path

1. MCP client (or any caller that doesn't pre-validate against the
   schema) sends \`{"query": "x", "timeout_secs": 0}\` to ca.search.
2. \`serde_json::from_value\` accepts the 0 → \`params.timeout_secs = 0\`.
3. \`cass_client_with_timeout(0)\` constructs a CassClient with
   \`timeout = Duration::from_secs(0)\` (cass.rs:781-784).
4. \`run_with_cx\` (cass.rs:961) calls
   \`timeout_with_cx(cx, Duration::from_secs(0), cmd.output()).await\`
   — a zero-duration timeout fires immediately, before \`cmd.output()\`
   has any chance to execute.
5. Returns \`CassError::Timeout { timeout_secs: 0 }\` to the MCP client
   on EVERY call. The client sees a confusing "cass timeout (0 secs)"
   error and may not realize the issue is its own \`timeout_secs: 0\`
   parameter rather than a slow cass binary.

Same defect shape as ft-t62hq (already fixed for wa.wait_for and
wa.send tools at lines 1149, 1873) — three sibling cass tools were
missed in that sweep.

## Severity rationale

- Silent UX failure: caller's request always times out without ever
  invoking the underlying tool.
- Reachable via any client that doesn't validate against the
  advertised schema (in practice: most language SDKs that just pass
  through user-supplied JSON, plus any test fixture that omits
  timeout_secs default-checking).
- Not a crash, not data corruption.

## Fix sketch

Mirror the ft-t62hq guard at the top of each \`call(\)\` for the three
cass tools:

\`\`\`rust
if params.timeout_secs == 0 {
    let envelope = McpEnvelope::<()>::error(
        MCP_ERR_INVALID_ARGS,
        "timeout_secs must be >= 1 (got 0)".to_string(),
        Some(
            "The ca.<tool> schema declares timeout_secs with \\
             minimum: 1; omit the field to use the default (15)."
                .to_string(),
        ),
        elapsed_ms(start),
    );
    return envelope_to_content(envelope);
}
\`\`\`

## Regression tests

Three integration-style tests at the params layer:
- \`ca_search_rejects_zero_timeout_secs\`
- \`ca_view_rejects_zero_timeout_secs\`
- \`ca_status_rejects_zero_timeout_secs\`

Each invokes the tool with \`timeout_secs: 0\` and asserts the response
is an Error envelope with \`code = MCP_ERR_INVALID_ARGS\` and the
"timeout_secs must be >= 1" message — proving the guard fires before
\`cass_client_with_timeout\` is ever called.

## Scope

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs\` only. No schema changes —
the schemas already declare \`minimum: 1\`. No changes to \`cass.rs\` —
\`with_timeout_secs(0)\` is a documented "instant timeout" primitive
that other callers may legitimately want.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane0","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:39:11.699057Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:49:15.687876Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:49:15.687498Z","close_reason":"Landed in db5bfeaf (fix(mcp_tools): reject timeout_secs=0 in ca.{search,view,status})","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

E0063 blocks cargo check/build of frankenterm-core lib.  {"id":"ft-up6u2","title":"[security] mission + tx contract loaders lack size cap — OOM vector for hostile repo .ft/mission/*.json","description":"# Bug

`mcp_load_mission_from_path` (mcp_missions.rs:257) and
`mcp_load_mission_tx_contract_from_path` (mcp_missions.rs:141) both
use bare `std::fs::read_to_string(path)` with no stat-before-read
size guard.

Pattern-pack loader has this exact guard (ft-05hfm, MAX_PACK_BYTES =
16 MiB). Mission files under `.ft/mission/*.json` have the same
trust property as patterns under `.ft/patterns/*.{yaml,json,toml}` —
a hostile repo can ship them, and they're loaded at MCP tool
invocation time.

# Attack

1. Hostile repo ships `.ft/mission/active.json` = 5 GiB of garbage.
2. Operator (or agent via wa.mission_state MCP) opens the project.
3. `wa.mission_state` → `mcp_load_mission_from_path` →
   `read_to_string` inhales 5 GiB into memory → watcher OOMs.

Same attack for `.ft/mission/tx-active.json` via wa.tx_show / wa.tx_run.

`resolve_workspace_scoped_path` (mcp_missions.rs:61) blocks
path-traversal but does NOT cap file size inside the workspace.

# Fix

Add `MAX_MISSION_BYTES` + `MAX_TX_CONTRACT_BYTES` constants (16 MiB
each, matching MAX_PACK_BYTES) with a stat-before-read check + a
second-line check after read, mirroring the pattern-pack loader
structure.

Both constants at 16 MiB because:
- mission JSON + 100 assignments ≈ 50-100 KiB realistically
- tx contract + plan ≈ similar
- 16 MiB = 200x headroom

# Tests

Regression tests similar to `load_pack_from_file_rejects_oversize_yaml`:
  - `mcp_load_mission_from_path_rejects_oversize_json`
  - `mcp_load_mission_tx_contract_from_path_rejects_oversize_json`","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:25:19.149935Z","created_by":"pane3-mission-cap","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:45:21.408462Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:45:21.408112Z","close_reason":"Landed in 3e2ae80c. Added MAX_MISSION_BYTES and MAX_TX_CONTRACT_BYTES (both 16 MiB, matching MAX_PACK_BYTES from ft-05hfm) + shared read_capped_file helper + 2 regression tests (load_tx_contract_rejects_oversize_json, load_mission_rejects_oversize_json). Preserved historical robot.tx_not_found / robot.mission_not_found error codes on NotFound branch. Full verification blocked on ft-golgl (pre-existing compile errors in diagnostic.rs, outcome.rs, workflows/handlers.rs under --features mcp); changes reviewed against the ft-05hfm pattern shipping on HEAD.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mcp","mission","security"]}
 {"id":"ft-uqlrq","title":"[mock] search/model2vec_embedder.rs:36 embed() is a stub that always errors","description":"search/model2vec_embedder.rs:36-38 has `fn embed(&self, _text: &str) -> Result<Vec<f32>, EmbedError> { // Stub: real implementation loads ONNX model; Err(EmbedError::ModelNotFound(...)) }`. The entire Embedder trait impl is a stub that always returns ModelNotFound. Zero production callers exist — tests in search/mod.rs and daemon/worker.rs explicitly verify model2vec is "retired" and rejected. Production behavior should be: either (a) implement real ONNX model loading if model2vec is still planned, or (b) delete the file entirely since it is dead code with no callers and tests actively reject it.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:32:43.678300Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:52:17.389463Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:52:17.389208Z","close_reason":"Shipped in af925d26. Audit confirmed the file was orphaned:
- not declared in search/mod.rs (no 'mod model2vec_embedder;' anywhere)
- zero constructors of Model2VecEmbedder outside the file itself
- three tests across the tree explicitly verify model2vec is RETIRED (never advertised as a tier, rejected as a selector, rejected by the embed worker): search::tests::advertised_embedder_tiers_never_reports_retired_model2vec_backend, search::daemon::worker::tests::build_embedder_rejects_retired_model2vec_selector, tests/semantic_backend_selection_integration.rs::embed_worker_rejects_retired_model2vec_selector

Decision rationale: the bead's two options (a/b) are 'wire up real ONNX impl' or 'surface via feature flag + clear error'. Both miss for orphan code with retirement tests in place:
- (a) would contradict those tests and re-expand a surface we deliberately shrank
- (b) only helps if there's a live caller who needs a clear error; there isn't one

The correct 'not supported in this build' signal for orphan code is absence. Delete the 40-line skeleton; a future model2vec revival will write fresh against whatever the Embedder trait looks like then. Also updated the stale 'e.g. Model2Vec distilled' comment on ManagedEmbedderStack.fast in orchestrator.rs to describe the Fast-tier slot generically + cross-link the retirement test.

The three retirement tests stay intact — they continue pinning the invariant.

Verification (rch exec -- env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/ft-p5-orch-target):
- cargo check -p frankenterm-core --lib --no-default-features: clean
- --features semantic-search build blocked by 13 pre-existing unrelated errors in web/sse.rs (select! macro fallout from the 9e1bd63b cfg-strip); my patch only removes a file + edits a doc comment so it cannot introduce compile errors

Note: the commit that carries the change is misleadingly titled 'docs(readme): correct runtime_compat boundary claim' — another agent's linter bundled my uncommitted model2vec deletion + orchestrator comment into its own commit. The 44 lines of change are correct (40 deletions in model2vec_embedder.rs + 4 lines in orchestrator.rs doc comment); only the commit title under-describes the scope.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-v0339","title":"[MEDIUM] integration_mux_checkpoint_topology post-rollback check only compares pane-id set, not tree shape","description":"Commit 30172171 fixed ft-olq2e by routing the post-rollback check through server.registry().snapshot() instead of the vacuous re-deserialization of initial_topo_json. However the new primary assertion at integration_mux_checkpoint_topology.rs still only compares PANE IDs:

  post_rollback_pane_ids_sorted == baseline_pane_ids

TopologySnapshot (crates/frankenterm-core/src/session_topology.rs) is a tree of windows → tabs → panes, with per-window position/size/title and per-tab pane layout metadata. The registry snapshot, filtered to LifecycleEntityKind::Pane, flattens all of that to a bag of pane u64 ids.

Consequence: a RemoteRequest::Rollback that restores the correct set of pane entities but (a) reparents them under the wrong tab, (b) drops a window or tab node, (c) corrupts the split tree under a tab, or (d) restores panes in the wrong order will still pass the assertion. Rollback tree-shape correctness is therefore still not covered by this test — the bead it closes was motivated precisely by false confidence in rollback behavior.

Expected fix:
- Reconstruct a post-rollback TopologySnapshot from the live server (registry + whatever stores tab/window edges) and compare it field-by-field against the pre-expansion baseline — at least window ids + tab ids + pane ids grouped by tab.
- Keep the pane-id-set assertion as a weaker redundant guard.

Regression risk: medium. The test name promises topology validation; reviewers relying on this bead's closure will assume tree-structure regressions are caught when in fact only pane existence is.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T07:02:21.495704Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T08:19:22.017561Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T08:19:22.017321Z","close_reason":"Strengthened rollback validation to require exact TopologySnapshot restoration, not just pane-id/fingerprint agreement","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mux","review","session-persistence","test-gap"]}
